easy.question: What is the overall aim of the Neighbourhood Management Policy?
easy.answer: To manage Midland Heart homes and surrounding areas so tenants feel safe and proud of where they live, working in partnership with tenants and local agencies to maintain clean, safe, well-managed neighbourhoods. :contentReference[oaicite:50]{index=50}

easy.question: How are abandoned properties treated?
easy.answer: They’re prioritised for action because abandonment is tenancy fraud and blights neighbourhoods; Midland Heart acts to bring homes back into use. :contentReference[oaicite:51]{index=51}

easy.question: What is Midland Heart’s position on items in communal areas?
easy.answer: Items must not be left in communal spaces; high-risk items (e.g., mobility scooters, e-bikes, motorbikes, fuel-driven machinery) will be removed and stored; fire doors must not be propped open; no smoking is permitted indoors. :contentReference[oaicite:52]{index=52}

easy.question: What responsibilities apply to vehicles on Midland Heart land?
easy.answer: Vehicles must be taxed and roadworthy; nuisance/abandoned vehicles may be removed. :contentReference[oaicite:53]{index=53}

easy.question: How quickly is offensive graffiti addressed?
easy.answer: Swiftly—graffiti on Midland Heart property/land is removed in line with service standards and criminal damage may be reported to Police. :contentReference[oaicite:54]{index=54}

easy.question: Who is responsible for trees in private gardens?
easy.answer: Customers; Midland Heart maintains trees only in its communal areas/estates under a risk-based programme and asset database. :contentReference[oaicite:55]{index=55}

medium.question: A tenant complains about cannabis smells in a corridor. How is this triaged and handled?
medium.answer: Apply a harm-centred, proportionate approach: assess aggravating factors (e.g., health impacts), decide whether it’s environmental ASB or a tenancy/household issue, and take proportionate action while managing expectations. :contentReference[oaicite:56]{index=56}

medium.question: A garden is repeatedly overgrown and attracting pests. What sequence of responses does the policy anticipate?
medium.answer: Advise the customer and allow a chance to remedy; persistent failure is a tenancy breach, enabling further action (including legal remedies). Address root causes (e.g., waste/fly-tipping) with partners while resolving pest issues. :contentReference[oaicite:57]{index=57}

medium.question: Tenants report corridor obstructions from buggies and ornaments. What should staff do on inspection?
medium.answer: Record and remove items from communal areas to protect means of escape; note any damage or safety equipment interference; escalate repairs; continue routine communal/estate inspections per the Environmental Services approach. :contentReference[oaicite:58]{index=58}

medium.question: There’s a noise dispute between neighbours over evening bass. When is it ASB vs. neighbourhood management?
medium.answer: Noise is triaged by type/time. Many domestic noises are neighbourhood-management issues (advice, good-neighbour agreements, practical mitigations like carpets/anti-vibration mats); sustained/targeted nuisance may be ASB. :contentReference[oaicite:59]{index=59}

medium.question: Who are typical partners for issues beyond Midland Heart’s control (e.g., street lighting, highways)?
medium.answer: Local Authorities and other landowners; Midland Heart collaborates to get non-landlord issues addressed swiftly and influence external neighbourhood services. :contentReference[oaicite:60]{index=60}

medium.question: How should customers report neighbourhood issues and how are social posts handled?
medium.answer: Customers are encouraged to report via the Customer Hub, but any channel is accepted (staff, phone, email, social); social posts get a private reply to protect confidentiality. :contentReference[oaicite:61]{index=61}

hard.question: Draft a joined-up plan for a block with: persistent stairwell obstructions (scooters, prams), fly-tipping near bins, and repeated corridor cannabis odours affecting an asthmatic tenant.
hard.answer: Immediate removal of communal area items and reinforce ‘no items’ rules; increase inspections/housekeeping checks. Clear fly-tipping in communal areas and liaise with the Local Authority about public land; encourage recycling and proper storage. For odours, apply a harm-centred, proportionate response: gather evidence, consider tenancy breaches vs. ASB thresholds, prioritise health impacts (e.g., the asthmatic), and use graduated responses (advice → warnings/enforcement as warranted); keep residents informed. :contentReference[oaicite:62]{index=62}

hard.question: A cluster of abandoned/nuisance vehicles is attracting crime. How should Midland Heart address this and reduce recurrence?
hard.answer: Identify and remove untaxed/unroadworthy vehicles on Midland Heart land; coordinate with Police/Local Authority on adjacent land; improve signage/lighting; communicate responsibilities to tenants and promote reporting routes; include hotspot checks in inspection programmes. :contentReference[oaicite:63]{index=63}

hard.question: Residents say ‘minor graffiti’ isn’t a priority, but it keeps spreading. Justify swift removal and outline the operating model.
hard.answer: The policy links graffiti to neighbourhood appearance and community confidence; swift removal discourages escalation and supports safety perceptions. Operate to service standards: prompt removal on Midland Heart land, report police crime where relevant, and escalate with partner landowners when off-land. :contentReference[oaicite:64]{index=64}

hard.question: Design an evidence pack for legal action against a tenant repeatedly leaving items in communal areas despite warnings.
hard.answer: Include inspection logs/photos showing obstructions over time; communications issued; tenancy clauses breached; any FRA/housekeeping findings; removal records; witness statements; and records of reasonable adjustments offered. :contentReference[oaicite:65]{index=65}

hard.question: How should Midland Heart embed learning and visibility for neighbourhood management performance?
hard.answer: Maintain inspection schedules/records, analyse complaint trends, feed learning into service standards, keep customers informed about actions taken on their reports, and review partnership protocols for faster responses to non-landlord issues. :contentReference[oaicite:66]{index=66}
